coverage_note: 37 importing nations, 8 chokepoints, 16 commodities. Crude oil (2709) uses Tier 1 bilateral data. All other commodities are Tier 3 aggregate allocations pending Eurostat/IEA bilateral pulls.

data_tiers.3: Aggregate — national totals allocated by corridor logic

2709.inelasticity_note: Origin-weighted: ~35% of French crude enters via Marseille/Fos (Mediterranean). Of that, Atlantic-origin crude transiting Gibraltar is ~65%. Le Havre/Dunkirk (Atlantic ports) do not use Gibraltar. Approx 22-25% of French crude is Gibraltar-captive.

Origin-weighted: ~25% of German crude imports enter via Mediterranean (TAL pipeline from Trieste) and transit Gibraltar. Remaining 75% arrives via North Sea/Rostock from North Sea/Russian/African origins.
Routing exclusion: Denmark imports crude via North Sea ports. No Gibraltar transit.
Routing exclusion: Finland crude arrives via North Sea/Atlantic ports, not Gibraltar.
Routing exclusion: Belgium crude arrives via North Sea/Atlantic ports, not Gibraltar.

2709.inelasticity_note: Origin-weighted: ~65% of Chinese crude imports are Hormuz-captive (Middle East: Saudi, Iraq, UAE, Kuwait, Oman). Russian ESPO (~22%) and West African crude (~6%) bypass Hormuz. Adj aligns with EIA ~128B Middle East-to-China crude flow.
2709.data_note: WITS 2023 T1 bilateral (270900). Total crude imports $337.645B. Hormuz-origin (excl. Oman): Saudi $53.877B + Iraq $35.211B + UAE $26.64B + Kuwait $15.219B + Qatar $6.532B = $137.479B (40.7%). Oman $25.039B EXCLUDED (Mina al-Fahal outside Hormuz Strait). Russia $60.679B (18.0%) via ESPO/Arctic, non-Hormuz. Malaysia $28.611B (8.5%) non-Hormuz; includes probable Iranian/Russian transshipment but route origin is Malaysian ports. Prior model dep 32.2% was denominator bug (country total used) + Oman incorrectly included.

2709.inelasticity_note: Origin-weighted: Brazilian refineries import some Middle East crude (~50%) but also North/West African, Russian, and domestic crude. Partial Hormuz dependency.

Revised ri=0.50 (from 0.90). Eastern Canadian refineries (Irving Oil Saint John, Valero Quebec City) do import Gulf-origin crude; dep=14.5% reflects genuine Hormuz fraction. 50% ri accounts for North Sea / West African misattribution in model.
Noise exclusion: dep=0% and adj<$10M.
Noise exclusion: dep=0% and adj<$10M.

EIA T1 bilateral data. Hormuz-origin: Saudi 349kbd + Iraq 213kbd + Kuwait 21kbd + UAE 17kbd + Qatar 4kbd = 604kbd / 6,489kbd total = 9.31%. ri=0.15: US has deep non-Hormuz diversification (Canada 60%, Mexico 11%, Colombia/Brazil/Guyana 8%) but Gulf Coast heavy-sour refinery configs limit substitution speed; 90-day contract lag assumed.
Japan crude is almost entirely Gulf-captive (95.3%). US and Ecuador provide <3% non-Gulf supply. No significant alternative crude supply chain exists at short notice.
2709.data_note: WITS 2023 T1 bilateral (270900). Total crude imports $80.885B. Hormuz-origin: Saudi $33.190B + UAE $31.815B + Kuwait $7.185B + Qatar $3.901B + Oman $0.935B + Bahrain $0.085B = $77.111B (95.3%). Non-Hormuz suppliers minimal: US $1.626B, Ecuador $0.994B, Australia $0.395B. Oman EXCLUDED: Mina al-Fahal terminal is outside Strait of Hormuz. Oman $0.935B removed. Corrected: $76.176B / $80.885B = 94.2%.

2709.inelasticity_note: Korea crude 72.1% Hormuz-captive. US provides 14.3% — meaningful diversification vs LPG (88% US) but crude cannot substitute at short notice. Kazakhstan via BTC/CPC bypasses Hormuz.
2709.data_note: WITS 2023 T1 bilateral (270900). Total crude imports $86.158B. Hormuz-origin: Saudi $30.570B + UAE $9.800B + Kuwait $8.306B + Iraq $7.345B + Qatar $5.624B + Oman $0.437B = $62.082B (72.1%). Non-Hormuz: US $12.317B (14.3%), Kazakhstan $3.260B (3.8%), Mexico $2.412B, Australia $2.030B. Oman EXCLUDED: Mina al-Fahal terminal is outside Strait of Hormuz. Oman $0.437B removed. Corrected: $61.645B / $86.158B = 71.5%.

2709.inelasticity_note: India crude 47.8% Hormuz-captive but Russia ($48.6B, 34.6%) provides large existing alternative. Short-term Hormuz disruption would stress Gulf volumes but Russia supply partially compensates. Net inelasticity lower than Japan/Korea.
2709.data_note: WITS 2023 T1 bilateral (270900). Total crude imports $140.386B. Hormuz-origin: Iraq $28.320B + Saudi $23.625B + UAE $8.632B + Kuwait $4.818B + Qatar $1.309B + Oman $0.453B = $67.157B (47.8%). Russia $48.640B (34.6%) is largest single supplier — post-2022 discount buying has diversified India away from Gulf. Also: US $5.618B, Nigeria $2.796B, Angola $2.344B, Mexico $2.045B. Oman EXCLUDED: Mina al-Fahal terminal is outside Strait of Hormuz. Oman $0.453B removed. Corrected: $66.704B / $140.386B = 47.5%.

2814.data_note: WITS 2023 T1. 281410 total $572M. Hormuz: Saudi $204M + Qatar $38M + Bahrain $11M  = $258M (45.0%). Indonesia $261M dominant via Makassar/east route, not Malacca. 281420 negligible. Oman EXCLUDED: Mina al-Fahal terminal is outside Hormuz Strait (Gulf of Oman side). Oman 5M removed. True Hormuz exposed = $253M / $572M = 44.2%.

WITS 2023 T1. 281410 $326M + 281420 $5M = $330M. Hormuz: Saudi $73M + Qatar $7M + UAE $4M + Bahrain $3M = $101M (30.5%). Indonesia $212M via east route, not Malacca. Oman EXCLUDED: Mina al-Fahal terminal is outside Hormuz Strait (Gulf of Oman side). Oman 14M removed. True Hormuz exposed = $87M / $330M = 26.4%.
WITS 2023 T1. 281410 $189M + 281420 $1M = $190M. Hormuz: Saudi $17M + Qatar $6M = $23M (12.0%). Malaysia $88M dominant (regional short-sea). 281420 negligible.

2814.inelasticity_note: EXCLUDED: adjVarB=$4.0M below noise floor ($5M threshold).

2814.data_note: WITS 2023 T1. 281410 $1,220M. Hormuz: Saudi $474M + Bahrain $205M + Qatar $62M + UAE $9M = $946M (77.5%). Indonesia $117M, China $60M, Malaysia $58M non-Hormuz. Oman EXCLUDED: Mina al-Fahal terminal is outside Hormuz Strait (Gulf of Oman side). Oman 196M removed. True Hormuz exposed = $750M / $1220M = 61.5%.

3102.data_note: WITS 2023 T1 bilateral (310210). Total urea imports $2.840B. Hormuz-origin: Oman $604.8M + Qatar $530.4M + Saudi $94.3M + Bahrain $62.4M + UAE $44.9M = $1.337B (47.1%). Non-Hormuz: Nigeria $474.6M, Russia $317.3M, Algeria $288.9M, Venezuela $208.3M. Prior model dep 9.8% was denominator bug (country total used instead of commodity total). Oman EXCLUDED (OQ Fertilizers/Sur terminal on Gulf of Oman, outside Hormuz). Oman $604.8M removed. Corrected Hormuz-exposed: $732.2M / $2839.8M = 25.8%.

WITS 2023 T1 bilateral (310210). Total urea imports $989M. Hormuz-origin: Saudi $384.3M + Qatar $167.8M + Oman $74.7M + UAE $36.8M + Bahrain $13.2M = $676.8M (68.4%). Non-Hormuz: Malaysia $204.3M, Brunei $65.2M, Indonesia $27.0M. Prior model dep 11.4% was denominator bug. Oman EXCLUDED (OQ Fertilizers/Sur terminal on Gulf of Oman, outside Hormuz). Oman $74.7M removed. Corrected Hormuz-exposed: $602.1M / $989.3M = 60.9%.

2601.inelasticity_note: Origin-weighted routing correction. Australian iron ore (65% of imports) bypasses Malacca via direct Pacific route. Brazilian iron ore (22%) routes via Cape of Good Hope. Only Indian Ocean-origin supply (South Africa, India, Mozambique) transits Malacca — estimated 10% of imports. Source: World Steel Association 2022 import origin data.

Origin-weighted routing correction. Australian iron ore (60% of imports) bypasses Malacca via direct Pacific route. Brazilian iron ore (25%) routes via Cape of Good Hope. Only Indian Ocean-origin supply (South Africa, India, Mozambique) transits Malacca — estimated 10% of imports. Source: World Steel Association 2022 import origin data.

Origin-weighted routing correction. Australian iron ore (57% of imports) bypasses Malacca via direct Pacific route. Brazilian iron ore (22%) routes via Cape of Good Hope. Only Indian Ocean-origin supply (South Africa, India, Mozambique) transits Malacca — estimated 12% of imports. Source: World Steel Association 2022 import origin data.

Origin-weighted routing correction. Australian iron ore (10% of imports) bypasses Malacca via direct Pacific route. Brazilian iron ore (0%) routes via Cape of Good Hope. Only Indian Ocean-origin supply (South Africa, India, Mozambique) transits Malacca — estimated 70% of imports. Source: World Steel Association 2022 import origin data.

2603.inelasticity_note: Origin-weighted: ~15% Malacca-captive (DRC/Zambia origin via Cape). Chilean/Peruvian origin (39%) via Pacific, Australian (~8%) and Indonesian (~7%) via Lombok/direct sea.
Origin-weighted: ~10% Malacca-captive (African copper). Chile/Philippines/Indonesia via Pacific or direct.
Origin-weighted: ~10% Malacca-captive (African origin). Remainder from Pacific-routing sources.
Routing correction: Philippines is a copper producer (Philex, OceanaGold). Domestic and regional; minimal Malacca dependency.
Routing correction: Spain imports copper via Atlantic (Chilean/African Atlantic ports, Suez). NOT Malacca-captive.
Routing correction: Germany copper imports via Atlantic/North Sea/Suez. NOT Malacca-captive.
Origin-weighted: ~12% from DRC via Cape/Malacca. Remainder Chilean/Philippine Pacific origin.
Origin-weighted: ~15% Malacca-captive (African origin). Malaysia has domestic copper smelting from imported concentrate.

2701.inelasticity_note: Origin-weighted routing correction. Australian coal (62% of imports) bypasses Malacca via direct Pacific route. Indonesian coal partially transits Malacca (~50% of Indonesian share). Estimated Malacca-captive fraction: 20% of total imports. Source: IEA Coal 2023, BP Statistical Review 2023.
2701.data_note: Corrected to 0%. WITS 2023 bilateral (270112): JPN coal suppliers are Australia, Indonesia, Canada, US, Russia — all Pacific Rim sources. These routes transit Makassar Strait / South China Sea / Philippine Sea to reach JPN, NOT Malacca Strait. Confirmed by CEPAL routing analysis: coal bound for Japan/Korea transits Lombok+Makassar+Philippine Sea. Large bulk carriers >230k DWT physically cannot transit Malacca (draft limits). Prior 55.8% was routing logic error in corridor allocation algorithm (incorrectly assigned Malacca to Pacific-routed suppliers).

2701.inelasticity_note: Origin-weighted routing correction. Australian coal (5% of imports) bypasses Malacca via direct Pacific route. Indonesian coal partially transits Malacca (~50% of Indonesian share). Estimated Malacca-captive fraction: 40% of total imports. Source: IEA Coal 2023, BP Statistical Review 2023.

Origin-weighted routing correction. Australian coal (55% of imports) bypasses Malacca via direct Pacific route. Indonesian coal partially transits Malacca (~50% of Indonesian share). Estimated Malacca-captive fraction: 22% of total imports. Source: IEA Coal 2023, BP Statistical Review 2023.
2701.data_note: Corrected to 0%. WITS 2023 bilateral (270112): KOR coal suppliers are Australia, Indonesia, Canada, US, Russia — all Pacific Rim sources. These routes transit Makassar Strait / South China Sea / Philippine Sea to reach KOR, NOT Malacca Strait. Confirmed by CEPAL routing analysis: coal bound for Japan/Korea transits Lombok+Makassar+Philippine Sea. Large bulk carriers >230k DWT physically cannot transit Malacca (draft limits). Prior 46.6% was routing logic error in corridor allocation algorithm (incorrectly assigned Malacca to Pacific-routed suppliers).

2701.inelasticity_note: Bilateral-validated. All major suppliers (AUS, IDN, RUS, ZAF, CAN, COL) route via Philippine Sea / Luzon Strait to Taiwan. Zero Malacca captive fraction.
2701.data_note: Corrected to 0%. Comtrade 2023 bilateral (HS 2701): TWN coal suppliers are Australia (59%), Indonesia (20%), Russia (10%), South Africa (5%), Canada (4%), Colombia (2%). All are Pacific Rim or Atlantic sources. These routes transit Philippine Sea / Luzon Strait to reach Taiwan, NOT Malacca Strait. Large bulk carriers >230k DWT physically cannot transit Malacca (draft limits). Prior 70.7% was routing logic error (incorrectly assigned Malacca to Pacific-routed suppliers).

2701.inelasticity_note: Routing-validated. AUS, IDN, RUS suppliers reach Vietnam via South China Sea. Zero Malacca captive fraction.
2701.data_note: Corrected to 0%. Comtrade 2023 (HS 2701): VNM coal suppliers are Australia (47%), Indonesia (29%), Russia (12%), Mozambique (5%), South Africa (3%). All route via South China Sea to Vietnam without transiting Malacca Strait. Australian/Indonesian coal enters South China Sea via Torres Strait or Makassar/Celebes, not Malacca. Prior 48.8% was routing logic error. Same class of error as JPN/KOR correction.

2701.inelasticity_note: Origin-weighted routing correction. Australian coal (20% of imports) bypasses Malacca via direct Pacific route. Indonesian coal partially transits Malacca (~50% of Indonesian share). Estimated Malacca-captive fraction: 60% of total imports. Source: IEA Coal 2023, BP Statistical Review 2023.
Routing-validated. Indonesia (98%) ships via Celebes/Sulu Sea. Philippines is east of Malacca. Zero Malacca captive fraction.
2701.data_note: Corrected to 0%. Comtrade 2023 (HS 2701): PHL coal is 97.9% Indonesian origin. Indonesian coal to Philippines routes via Celebes Sea / Sulu Sea, east of Malacca Strait. Philippines is geographically east of Malacca — no inbound coal route transits it. Prior 42.3% was routing logic error. Same class as JPN/KOR/TWN/VNM corrections.

2701.inelasticity_note: Origin-weighted routing correction. Australian coal (10% of imports) bypasses Malacca via direct Pacific route. Indonesian coal partially transits Malacca (~50% of Indonesian share). Estimated Malacca-captive fraction: 70% of total imports. Source: IEA Coal 2023, BP Statistical Review 2023.

Origin-weighted routing correction. Australian coal (20% of imports) bypasses Malacca via direct Pacific route. Indonesian coal partially transits Malacca (~50% of Indonesian share). Estimated Malacca-captive fraction: 55% of total imports. Source: IEA Coal 2023, BP Statistical Review 2023.

2710.inelasticity_note: Data magnitude alert: Australia refined petroleum varB=$32.45B is estimated 5-6x actual import value (~$5-6B/yr from Singapore/Asia). Apply ri=0.83 pending data audit. Singapore-origin petroleum IS Malacca-captive (adj ~$5.5B); other sources include Middle East (Malacca-captive) and US/European (not Malacca).
Microstate exclusion: Andorra has no significant seaborne trade; rows are Comtrade re-export artefacts.

2814.data_note: WITS 2023 T1 bilateral (281410). Gulf-origin ammonia ($258M of $572M total) transits BOTH Hormuz AND Malacca en route to Korea. Malacca dep = Hormuz-origin fraction = 45.0%. Indonesia $261M dominant but routes via Makassar Strait/SCS, bypasses Malacca. Oman EXCLUDED (outside Hormuz). Revised Malacca-exposed Gulf fraction: $253M / $572M = 44.2%.

WITS 2023 T1 bilateral (281410). Gulf-origin ammonia ($101M of $330M total) transits BOTH Hormuz AND Malacca en route to China. Malacca dep = Hormuz-origin fraction = 30.5%. Indonesia $212M dominant but routes via Makassar Strait/SCS, bypasses Malacca. Oman EXCLUDED (outside Hormuz). Revised Malacca-exposed Gulf fraction: $87M / $330M = 26.4%.

WITS 2023 T1 bilateral (281410). Gulf-origin ammonia (Saudi $14M + Qatar $9M = $23M of $191M) transits Malacca to reach Thailand. Malacca dep = 12.0%. Malaysia $88M dominant (regional, no Malacca), Indonesia $31M via regional straits.

3102.data_note: Corrected from T3 16.7% (denominator bug) to 62.1% T2. Comtrade 2023 bilateral (HS 3102): THA urea total imports ,070M. Malacca-captive suppliers: Saudi Arabia 84M (36%), Qatar 68M (16%), Oman 5M (7%), UAE 7M (3%) = 64M total. All route Hormuz -> Indian Ocean -> Malacca -> Gulf of Thailand. Oman excluded from Hormuz captive but IS Malacca-captive for Thailand. varB corrected to bash.664B (exposed value, not total imports).

Corrected from T3 5.5% (denominator bug) to 17.4% T2. Comtrade 2023 (HS 3102): PHL urea total imports 79M. Malacca-captive suppliers: Qatar 2M (9%), Saudi Arabia 0M (5%), Norway/Partner_579 9M (3%) = 01M total. These route Indian Ocean -> Malacca -> South China Sea -> Philippines. China (29%), Indonesia (20%), Malaysia (19%) route via South China Sea without Malacca transit. varB corrected to bash.101B.

Corrected from T3 0.8% (denominator bug) to 19.3% T2. Comtrade 2023 (HS 3102): KOR urea total imports 48M. Malacca-captive suppliers: Qatar 7M (13%), Saudi Arabia 0M (6%) = 7M. These route Hormuz -> Indian Ocean -> Malacca -> Korea. China (59%) routes via East China Sea direct, no Malacca transit. varB corrected to bash.067B.

Corrected to 0%. Comtrade 2023 (HS 3102): VNM urea total imports 47M. Dominant suppliers: China 55% (South China Sea, no Malacca), Indonesia 12% (Java Sea -> South China Sea, no Malacca), Brunei/P96 10% (South China Sea, no Malacca). No material Malacca-transiting supplier identified. Prior 2.3% was denominator bug.

Corrected to 0%. Comtrade 2023 (HS 3102): IDN urea total imports 79M. Dominant suppliers: China 44% (South China Sea -> Java Sea, no Malacca transit), Korea 11%, Vietnam 9%, Russia 7% (Indian Ocean -> Sunda/Lombok Strait, not Malacca). Prior 1.2% was denominator bug.

2601.inelasticity_note: Capesize vessels cannot transit Panama. Standard Brazil-China iron ore route is Cape of Good Hope eastward. Routing error.
Capesize vessels cannot transit Panama. Brazil-Japan iron ore standard route is Cape of Good Hope. Routing error.
Capesize vessels cannot transit Panama. Cape of Good Hope is standard route from Brazilian/Australian iron ore to Korea. Routing error.
Argentina iron ore via Panama: 12.7x ACP total northbound iron ore volume. No commercial route justification. Routing error.
Canada iron ore via Panama: 10.2x ACP total. Routing error.
Indonesia iron ore via Panama: geographic routing error. Indonesia is Pacific-facing; iron ore imports would not transit Panama.

2603.inelasticity_note: China copper ores via Panama: routing error. Chilean/Peruvian copper to China goes direct Pacific, not through Panama eastbound. ACP southbound copper ($11.49B) serves Atlantic smelters, not Asian ones.

2701.inelasticity_note: Round 4 (ACP FY2023 calibration): 12% of Japan's HS 2701 imports estimated to transit Panama Canal. Remainder via Australia 45% (Pacific), Indonesia 25% (Pacific). ACP cap=2.23B.
Round 4 (ACP FY2023 calibration): 15% of China's HS 2701 imports estimated to transit Panama Canal. Remainder via alternative routes. ACP cap=2.23B.
Round 4 (ACP FY2023 calibration): 18% of South Korea's HS 2701 imports estimated to transit Panama Canal. Remainder via alternative routes. ACP cap=2.23B.
Round 4 (ACP FY2023 calibration): 5% of Brazil's HS 2701 imports estimated to transit Panama Canal. Remainder via Colombia/South Africa (Atlantic). ACP cap=2.23B.
Round 4 (ACP FY2023 calibration): 3% of Canada's HS 2701 imports estimated to transit Panama Canal. Remainder via domestic exporter. ACP cap=2.23B.
Round 4 (ACP FY2023 calibration): 25% of Chile's HS 2701 imports estimated to transit Panama Canal. Remainder via Colombia Pacific + US Gulf via Panama 25%. ACP cap=2.23B.
Round 4 (ACP FY2023 calibration): 12% of Taiwan's HS 2701 imports estimated to transit Panama Canal. Remainder via alternative routes. ACP cap=2.23B.
Round 4 (ACP FY2023 calibration): 3% of Argentina's HS 2701 imports estimated to transit Panama Canal. Remainder via alternative routes. ACP cap=2.23B.

Round 4 (ACP FY2023 calibration): 2% of Indonesia's HS 2701 imports estimated to transit Panama Canal. Remainder via alternative routes. ACP cap=2.23B. EXCLUDED: adjVarB=$2.9M below noise floor ($5M threshold).

Round 4 (ACP FY2023 calibration): 3% of Malaysia's HS 2701 imports estimated to transit Panama Canal. Remainder via alternative routes. ACP cap=2.23B. EXCLUDED: adjVarB=$3.5M below noise floor ($5M threshold).
Round 4 (ACP FY2023 calibration): 5% of Vietnam's HS 2701 imports estimated to transit Panama Canal. Remainder via alternative routes. ACP cap=2.23B.
Noise exclusion: dep=0% and adj<$10M.

2709.inelasticity_note: VLCCs cannot transit Panama Canal. Crude oil Panama dependency is a routing error. ACP FY2023 total crude northbound = $0.53B; model China row alone was $34.3B (64x overstatement).
